items.title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
items.content: Prevent cascading failures by wrapping external calls. When failures exceed threshold, open circuit and fail fast. Auto-retry after timeout.

items.title: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)
items.content: Separate read and write models. Optimize each independently. Use when read/write patterns differ significantly or need different scaling.

items.title: Event Sourcing
items.content: Store state changes as events, not current state. Enables audit trails, time travel debugging, and event replay. Pairs well with CQRS.

items.title: Saga Pattern
items.content: Manage distributed transactions with compensating actions. Each step has a rollback. Use choreography (events) or orchestration (coordinator).

items.title: Strangler Fig Pattern
items.content: Gradually migrate legacy systems by routing traffic to new implementation incrementally. Reduces risk, enables continuous delivery during migration.

items.title: Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling
items.content: Horizontal: Add more machines (better fault tolerance, unlimited scaling). Vertical: Bigger machine (simpler, but limited and single point of failure).

items.title: Caching Strategies
items.content: Cache-aside: App manages cache. Write-through: Update DB and cache together. Write-behind: Update cache first, DB async. Choose based on consistency needs.

items.title: Database Sharding
items.content: Partition data across multiple DBs. Horizontal partitioning by key (user ID, region). Increases throughput but adds complexity. Plan shard key carefully.

items.title: Load Balancing Strategies
items.content: Round-robin: Simple rotation. Least connections: Route to least busy. Consistent hashing: Minimize reshuffling. Health checks: Avoid failed nodes.

items.title: Asynchronous Processing
items.content: Offload long-running tasks to queues. Improves response times, enables retry logic, and decouples services. Use for emails, reports, batch jobs.

items.title: Microservices vs Monolith
items.content: Microservices: Independent deploy, scale, tech choice. Cost: complexity, network overhead, distributed debugging. Start monolith, split when needed.

items.title: SQL vs NoSQL
items.content: SQL: ACID, complex queries, relationships. NoSQL: Flexible schema, horizontal scaling, eventual consistency. Choose based on data structure and query patterns.

items.title: Consistency vs Availability (CAP)
items.content: Can't have both during partition. CP: Strong consistency, reject writes when partitioned. AP: Always available, eventual consistency. Choose based on business needs.

items.title: Normalization vs Denormalization
items.content: Normalize: Reduce redundancy, easier updates. Denormalize: Faster reads, duplicate data. Denormalize for read-heavy, normalize for write-heavy.

items.title: Build vs Buy
items.content: Build: Full control, exact fit, learning. Buy: Faster, maintained, proven. Build core differentiators, buy commodities. Consider total cost of ownership.

items.title: ADR (Architecture Decision Record)
items.content: Document: Context, Decision, Consequences. Keep it short. Version control it. Immutable - don't edit, supersede. Makes decisions transparent and reversible.

items.title: RFC (Request for Comments)
items.content: Propose significant changes with: Problem, Proposal, Alternatives, Open Questions. Gather async feedback. Builds consensus and catches issues early.

items.title: DACI Framework
items.content: Driver: Owns decision. Approver: Final say. Contributors: Input. Informed: Notified. Clarifies roles, speeds decisions, reduces politics.

items.title: One-way vs Two-way Doors
items.content: One-way: Hard to reverse (architecture, data models). Slow, careful. Two-way: Easy to reverse (UI, configs). Fast, experimental. Know which you're opening.

items.title: Data-Driven Decisions
items.content: Gather metrics before deciding. A/B test when possible. Define success criteria upfront. Avoid HiPPO (Highest Paid Person's Opinion). Let data speak.

items.title: Writing Design Docs
items.content: Start with TL;DR. State problem clearly. Propose solution with diagrams. List alternatives considered. Define success metrics. Keep it concise.

items.title: Explaining Complex Topics
items.content: Use analogies. Start high-level, drill down. Tailor to audience (exec vs engineer). Use visuals. Check understanding. Avoid jargon unless necessary.

items.title: Stakeholder Management
items.content: Understand their goals. Speak their language (business value > tech details). Regular updates prevent surprises. Manage expectations. Say no with alternatives.

items.title: Giving Technical Feedback
items.content: Be specific, not vague. Focus on code, not person. Explain why, don't just prescribe. Ask questions. Praise good work. Use 'we' not 'you'.

items.title: Escalating Issues
items.content: Escalate early when blocked. Come with options, not just problems. Be clear about urgency and impact. Document context. Follow up promptly.

items.title: SOLID - Single Responsibility
items.content: A class should have one reason to change. Separate concerns. Makes code easier to understand, test, and modify. Example: Separate data access from business logic.

items.title: SOLID - Open/Closed Principle
items.content: Open for extension, closed for modification. Use interfaces, abstract classes, composition. Add features without changing existing code. Reduces regression risk.

items.title: SOLID - Liskov Substitution
items.content: Subtypes must be substitutable for base types. Don't break parent contracts. Prevents unexpected behavior. If it looks like a duck but needs batteries, wrong abstraction.

items.title: DRY - Don't Repeat Yourself
items.content: Every piece of knowledge should have single source. Extract common logic. But: Don't abstract too early. Similar code isn't always duplicate knowledge.

items.title: KISS - Keep It Simple
items.content: Simplest solution that works. Avoid premature optimization. Complexity is the enemy of reliability. Can a junior developer understand this in 6 months?

items.title: YAGNI - You Aren't Gonna Need It
items.content: Don't build features you don't need now. Solve today's problems. Future-proofing often wastes time. Build for change, not for every possible scenario.

items.title: Fail Fast Principle
items.content: Validate early, throw exceptions quickly. Don't let bad data propagate. Makes debugging easier. Use assertions, type checking, input validation at boundaries.

items.title: Code Reviews Best Practices
items.content: Review small PRs (<400 lines). Check: logic, security, tests, performance, readability. Automate style checks. Be timely. Learn from others' code.

items.title: Testing Strategy
items.content: Test pyramid: Many unit, some integration, few E2E. Test behavior, not implementation. Mock external dependencies. Keep tests fast. Treat test code as production code.

items.title: Technical Debt Management
items.content: Make it visible. Track in backlog. Allocate 20% time for cleanup. Don't let it compound. Sometimes debt is strategic - document it. Pay high-interest debt first.
